content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Mark Paxson 
General Counsel 
915 Capitol Mall, Suite 110 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-046 
Dear Mr. Paxson: 
April 7, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Treasurer John Chiang regarding 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
Is compensation received by a board member of the Federal Home Loan Bank of San 
Francisco (the "FHLB-SF") potentially reportable and disqualifying income under the Act or salary 
from a governmental agency? 
CONCLUSION 
The FHLB-SF is not a governmental agency for purposes of the Act. Accordingly, 
compensation received from the entity is potentially reportable and disqualifying. 
FACTS 
The FHLB-SF is a regional bank created with passage of the Federal Home Loan Bank Act 
through which Congress created the Federal Home Loan Bank system (the "FHLB System") in 
1932. The purpose of the system was to provide a reserve banking system to assist thrift institutions 
and the housing finance industry to help rescue homeowners through the Great Depression. The 
FHLB System consists of eleven regional banks. The FHLB-SF covers California, Nevada, and 
Arizona. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-046 
Page No. 2 
The FHLB-SF is a government sponsored enterprise. Like the other federal home loan 
banks, it is established as a cooperative. Private financial institutions must be members and invest in 
the FHLB-SF to be able to access the services and products it offers. The FHLB-SF is funded 
entirely by the capital contributions of its members, the returns it makes on its investments, and 
income from its products and services. It does not receive any funding from the federal government 
or any other government source. 
The FHLB System is overseen by the Federal Housing Finance Agency (the "Housing 
Agency"), an independent federal agency that also oversees other federally created government 
sponsored enterprises in the housing sector such as Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, and is governed 
by the Federal Home Loan Bank Act and regulations promulgated by the Housing Agency. Among 
other things, the Federal Home Loan Bank Act and implementing regulations, provide for the 
following: 
• The Housing Agency determines the number of federal home loan banks, as well as the 
jurisdiction of each bank and the location of each bank's offices. 
• The regulations prescribe capitalization standards for each federal home loan bank, 
requiring each bank meet specified leverage requirements, risk based capital requirements, 
and stock issuance requirements. 
• The Housing Agency is responsible for determining the number of directors for the board of 
directors for each federal home loan bank on an annual basis. 
• The Housing Agency establishes the uses, priorities, limitations, and standards for the use of 
advances by the federal home loan banks. 
• The federal home loan banks must comply with a number of reporting requirements. 
• The federal home loan banks may be depositaries of public money and may also be 
employed as financial agents of the government. 
According to the FHLB-SF, its "primary business is providing competitively priced, 
collateralized loans, known as advances, to our members and certain qualifying housing associates." 
Members use these advances to "support their mortgage loan portfolios, lower their funding costs, 
facilitate asset-liability management, reduce on-balance sheet liquidity, offer a wider range of 
mortgage products to their customers, and improve profitability." Besides its "primary business," 
the FHLB-SF also operates several grant programs, the funds for which are generated by profits 
generated by the FHLB-SF's other activities, including: (1) grants made to community-based 
affordable housing partners, (2) grants made to very low to moderate income first-time homebuyers 
for down payment assistance, (3) matching grants made to very low to moderate income first time 
home buyers in conjunction with other programs, and (4) grants to fund innovative economic 
development projects. The FHLB-SF also manages several other credit programs that provide 
assistance to member banks in financing community development and housing projects. 


File No. A-16-046 
Page No. 3 
The board of directors of the FHLB-SF is made up of member directors (representing 
member banks) and independent directors. Each year, the Housing Agency designates the total 
number of director positions for FHLB-SF, as well as the other federal home loan banks. The 
number of independent director positions must be at least two-fifths of the number of member 
director positions and at least two of the independent directors must represent the "public interest." 
As a result, for example, in 2014, the Housing Agency required that the FHLB-SF Board of 
Directors consist of eight member directors and six independent directors, with two of those 
directors representing the public interest. Candidates for the independent director positions are 
nominated by the FHLB-SF Board of Directors, following consultation with FHLB-SF's Advisory 
Council and review by the Housing Agency. The member banks then vote for the nominated 
individuals in proportion to their interest in the FHLB-SF. Independent directors must not be an 
officer of any federal home loan bank or an officer, director, or employee of any bank member. 
Public interest directors must also comply with specified minimum levels of experience in 
identified industries or professions. Members of the board of directors of the FHLB-SF receive 
compensation for their service. For 2016, compensation ranges from $80,000 - $115,000. 
With respect to federal law, it is unclear whether the FHLB-SF is treated as a government 
entity. For instance, the Government Corporation Control Act specifically identifies the Federal 
Home Loan Banks as "government corporations" subject to certain budget, audit, and accounting 
requirements and, according to FHLB-SF, its retirement plans are recognized by the Internal 
Revenue Service and federal Department of Labor as "governmental plans." However, it is your 
understanding that the FHLB System has taken the position that it is not an agency covered by the 
Freedom of Information Act. In addition, case law that looks at whether government corporations 
are a part of the government have looked to whether a majority of the corporation's board of 
directors was appointed by the government in determining whether such a corporation is a part of 
the U.S. government. Applying that test to FHLB-SF, at least one federal district court has 
concluded that a federal home loan bank is not a part of the federal government. (Xiangyuan Zhu v. 
Fed. Hous. Fin. Bd. (2005) 389 F. Supp. 2d 1253, 1281.) 
ANALYSIS 
Under the Act's provisions, income does not include "salary and reimbursements for 
expenses or per diem, and social security, disability, or other similar benefit payments received 
from a state, local, or federal government agency .... " (Section 82030.) Because of this exception, 
salary from a governmental agency is not reportable under the Act (see Sections 87200 et seq. and 
87300 et seq.) and an official who receives salary from a governmental agency is not disqualified 
from taking part in a decision with a financial effect on the agency barring a separate effect on the 
official's interest such as a reasonably foreseeable material effect on the official's personal finances 
(see Section 87103; Regulations 18700 et seq.). 
In most instances, the determination of whether an entity is a governmental agency is 
relatively straightforward. However, the determination of whether the FHLB-SF is a "federal 
governmental agency" does not fit the typical pattern and you have provided ample facts 
demonstrating that the FHLB-SF shares characteristics of both a private and governmental entity. 
Turning to the Act for guidance, the Act does not further define "federal governmental agency." 
However, Section 81003 does provide a broad mandate that the Act "should be liberally construed 


File No. A-16-046 
Page No. 4 
to accomplish its purposes" and the enumerated purposes of the Act include the following under 
Section 81002( c): 
"Assets and income of public officials which may be materially affected by 
their official actions should be disclosed and in appropriate circumstances the 
officials should be disqualified from acting in order that conflicts of interest may be 
avoided." 
Moreover, while not binding for purposes of the Act, it is also highly persuasive that Federal 
Home Loan Banks are not considered federal actors. Under federal case law, a corporate entity is a 
federal actor "only if (I) the government created the corporate entity by special law, (2) the 
government created the entity to further governmental objectives, and (3) the government retains 
permanent authority to appoint a majority of the directors of the corporation," and "although 
Congress created the Federal Home Loan Banks to further federal governmental objectives, the 
government appoints only six of the 14 directors for each regional bank." Accordingly, a regional 
Federal Home Loan Bank is not a federal actor. (Xiangyuan Zlzu v. Fed. Hous. Fin. Bd. (2005) 389 
F. Supp. 2d 1253, 1281.) 
Consistent with relevant federal case law and the Act's liberal construction to ensure that 
assets and income of public officials which may be materially affected by their official actions 
should be disclosed, the FHLB-SF is not a governmental agency for purposes of the Act. 
Accordingly, compensation received from the entity is potentially reportable and disqualifying. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
BGL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Brian G. Lau 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is compensation received by a board member of the Federal Home Loan Bank of San 
Francisco (the "FHLB-SF") potentially reportable and disqualifying income under the Act or salary 
from a governmental agency?

CONCLUSION: The FHLB-SF is not a governmental agency for purposes of the Act. Accordingly, 
compensation received from the entity is potentially reportable and disqualifying.
